Genes.biosynthesis_catabolism_transport_regula: Biosynthesis (NAD+ de novo pathway; upstream metabolic checkpoint controlling defense metabolism)
Genes.causal_chain_text: qs-2 point mutation in QS reduces NAD+ content, causing NAD dyshomeostasis. This leads to impaired photosynthesis, chloroplastic 1O2 burst, activation of LOX2-mediated JA biosynthesis, and hyperaccumulation of both aliphatic and indolic GSLs. Complementation with QS gene (COM1/COM2) or exogenous NA supplementation restores NAD+ levels, GSL contents, and growth phenotype to Col-0 levels.
Genes.directionality: qs-2 mutation → ↓NAD+ → ↑1O2 burst → ↑LOX2 activity → ↑13-HPOT, 12-OPDA, JA, JA-Ile → ↑aliphatic and indolic GSLs; complementation or NA treatment restores GSLs and NAD+ to Col-0 levels
Genes.evidence_type: Genetic perturbation (point mutation + complementation + chemical rescue) + targeted metabolite quantification (LC-MS)
Genes.final_nutrient_products_affected: Glucosinolates (aliphatic: 4BTEY, 4MSOB; indolic: I3M, 4MOI3M); also NAD+ content and chlorophyll
Genes.genotype_lines: qs-2 (NAD+ deficient mutant), COM1, COM2 (complementation lines)
Genes.intermediate_metabolites_affected: NAD+, NADH, JA, JA-Ile, 13-HPOT, 12-OPDA, α-linolenic acid, singlet oxygen (1O2)
Genes.pathway: De novo NAD+ biosynthesis pathway (aspartate → quinolinate → NAD+), chloroplast
Genes.perturbation_type: Point mutation (qs-2); genetic complementation (COM1, COM2); exogenous nicotinic acid (NA) supplementation to rescue salvage pathway
Genes.quantitative_effect: qs-2 shows significantly reduced NAD+ content; GSLs (I3M, 4MOI3M, 4BTEY, 4MSOB) hyperaccumulated in qs-2 and restored to Col-0 levels in COM1/COM2 and after NA treatment (Fig. 1d–k)
Genes.reaction_catalyzed: Synthesis of quinolinate, the precursor for de novo NAD+ biosynthesis
Genes.tissue_stage: Rosette leaves, 3-week-old plants grown in soil

Genes.biosynthesis_catabolism_transport_regula: Biosynthesis (key enzyme initiating JA biosynthesis from α-LeA; rate-limiting step activated by 1O2)
Genes.causal_chain_text: LOX2 is the prominent 13-lipoxygenase in rosette leaves catalyzing α-LeA to 13-HPOT, the first committed step of JA biosynthesis. In qs-2, LOX2 protein is highly accumulated. CRISPR knockout of LOX2 in qs-2 background leads to α-LeA accumulation, marked reduction of 13-HPOT, 12-OPDA, JA, and restoration of GSLs to Col-0 levels. DCMU-induced 1O2 activates JA biosynthesis in Col-0 but is significantly attenuated in lox2-c1, confirming LOX2 as the core enzyme in 1O2-mediated JA biosynthesis.
Genes.directionality: LOX2 knockout → ↑α-LeA accumulation, ↓13-HPOT, ↓12-OPDA, ↓JA → ↓GSLs in qs-2 restored to Col-0 levels; DCMU-induced 1O2 → ↑JA pathway metabolites in Col-0 but significantly less in lox2-c1
Genes.evidence_type: Genetic perturbation (CRISPR KO) + targeted metabolite quantification + immunoblotting (protein level) + chemical treatment (DCMU)
Genes.final_nutrient_products_affected: Glucosinolates (aliphatic and indolic GSLs: I3M, 4MOI3M, 4BTEY, 4MSOB); JA, JA-Ile
Genes.intermediate_metabolites_affected: α-linolenic acid (α-LeA, substrate accumulates upon LOX2 loss), 13-HPOT (product decreases), 12-OPDA, JA, JA-Ile
Genes.pathway: Jasmonate biosynthesis pathway: α-linolenic acid (α-LeA) → 13-HPOT → 12-OPDA → JA → JA-Ile, chloroplast
Genes.perturbation_type: CRISPR-Cas9 knockout in Col-0 (lox2-c1, lox2-c2) and qs-2 (qs-2lox2-c1, qs-2lox2-c2) backgrounds; DCMU treatment (induces 1O2) in Col-0 vs lox2-c1
Genes.quantitative_effect: Loss of LOX2 in qs-2 markedly reduced JA content with hyperaccumulation of α-LeA; GSL levels in qs-2lox2 restored to Col-0 levels (Fig. 4j–n). Under DCMU treatment, lox2-c1 showed significantly higher α-LeA and lower downstream JA products vs Col-0 (Fig. 5j–n)

Genes.causal_chain_text: CRISPR knockout of JA biosynthetic gene AOS in qs-2 background (qs-2aos) blocked JA biosynthesis, resulting in diminished JA, JA-Ile, and GSL levels comparable to Col-0. The double mutant also showed rescued growth, demonstrating that JA biosynthesis is required for the defense metabolic reprogramming and growth penalty in qs-2.
Genes.directionality: AOS knockout in qs-2 → ↓JA, ↓JA-Ile → ↓GSLs to Col-0 levels; rescued growth phenotype
Genes.evidence_type: Genetic perturbation (CRISPR KO) + targeted metabolite quantification + growth phenotyping
Genes.pathway: Jasmonate biosynthesis pathway: 13-HPOT → allene oxide → 12-OPDA → JA, chloroplast
Genes.perturbation_type: CRISPR-Cas9 knockout of AOS in qs-2 background (qs-2aos)
Genes.quantitative_effect: qs-2aos double mutant shows diminished JA and JA-Ile levels and GSL contents comparable to Col-0 (Fig. 3b); significantly larger rosette diameter than qs-2 (Fig. 3c)
Genes.reaction_catalyzed: Conversion of 13-HPOT to allene oxide (upstream of 12-OPDA in JA biosynthesis)

Genes.biosynthesis_catabolism_transport_regula: Regulation (JA signaling receptor; positive feedback on JA biosynthesis)
Genes.causal_chain_text: Blocking JA signal transduction by coi1-2 mutation in qs-2 background (qs-2coi1-2) restored GSL levels to Col-0, rescued growth, and reduced insect resistance. JA biosynthetic genes were downregulated and JA pathway intermediates (13-HPOT, 12-OPDA, JA) were reduced, indicating positive feedback of JA signaling on its own biosynthesis is important for the full defense response in qs-2.
Genes.directionality: COI1 loss in qs-2 → ↓JA signaling → ↓JA-responsive genes → ↓GSLs to Col-0 levels; ↑growth (larger rosettes); ↓insect resistance
Genes.evidence_type: Genetic perturbation (loss-of-function cross) + targeted metabolite quantification + insect bioassay + qRT-PCR
Genes.intermediate_metabolites_affected: JA signaling output; downstream JA-responsive gene expression; also affects JA biosynthesis via positive feedback (13-HPOT, 12-OPDA, JA reduced in qs-2coi1-2)
Genes.pathway: Jasmonate signaling pathway (JA receptor/co-receptor in SCF-COI1 complex)
Genes.perturbation_type: Loss-of-function mutation coi1-2 crossed with qs-2 to generate qs-2coi1-2
Genes.quantitative_effect: qs-2coi1-2 has GSL levels comparable to Col-0 (Fig. 3b); significantly larger rosettes than qs-2 (Fig. 3c); reduced insect resistance—larvae fed on qs-2coi1-2 gained more weight than on qs-2 (Fig. 3d, e). Also reduced 13-HPOT, 12-OPDA, JA in qs-2coi1-2 vs qs-2 (Supplementary Fig. 5k–n)
Genes.reaction_catalyzed: JA-Ile receptor; mediates JA signal transduction by targeting JAZ repressors for degradation

Genes.causal_chain_text: Overexpression of myrosinase BGLU28 in qs-2 background hydrolyzed accumulated GSLs, significantly reducing GSL levels. This eliminated the insect resistance phenotype of qs-2 (H. armigera larvae grew larger), while the small growth phenotype was unchanged, demonstrating that GSL accumulation (not other qs-2 effects) is responsible for insect resistance.
Genes.directionality: BGLU28 overexpression in qs-2 → ↓GSL levels significantly; ↓insect resistance (larvae grew larger on BGLU28-OE2/qs-2 leaves vs qs-2); growth phenotype (small) unchanged
Genes.evidence_type: Genetic perturbation (overexpression) + targeted metabolite quantification + insect bioassay
Genes.quantitative_effect: BGLU28-OE lines in qs-2 accumulate significantly less GSLs (Fig. 2a); insect larvae fed on BGLU28-OE2/qs-2 grew significantly larger than those on qs-2 (Fig. 2b, c)

Genes.causal_chain_text: CRISPR knockout of MYB28 and MYB29 in qs-2 background abolished aliphatic GSLs while retaining indolic GSLs. This eliminated insect resistance, demonstrating that aliphatic GSLs (regulated by MYB28/29) play a dominant role in conferring insect (H. armigera) resistance in qs-2.
Genes.directionality: MYB28/MYB29 knockout in qs-2 → ↓aliphatic GSLs (abolished); indolic GSLs retained → ↓insect resistance (lost)
Genes.evidence_type: Genetic perturbation (CRISPR KO) + targeted metabolite quantification + insect bioassay
Genes.pathway: Transcriptional regulation of aliphatic glucosinolate biosynthesis
Genes.perturbation_type: CRISPR-Cas9 knockout of MYB28 and MYB29 in qs-2 background
Genes.quantitative_effect: qs-2myb2829 triple mutants lack aliphatic GSLs while retaining indolic GSLs (Fig. 2d); insect resistance eliminated—larvae grew as large as on Col-0 (Fig. 2e, f)
Genes.reaction_catalyzed: Transcription factors activating aliphatic GSL biosynthetic genes

Genes.causal_chain_text: CRISPR knockout of MYB34 and MYB51 in qs-2 background abolished indolic GSLs while retaining aliphatic GSLs. Insect resistance was maintained, demonstrating that indolic GSLs (regulated by MYB34/51) are not the dominant contributors to insect resistance against H. armigera.
Genes.directionality: MYB34/MYB51 knockout in qs-2 → ↓indolic GSLs (abolished); aliphatic GSLs retained → insect resistance maintained
Genes.evidence_type: Genetic perturbation (CRISPR KO) + targeted metabolite quantification + insect bioassay
Genes.pathway: Transcriptional regulation of indolic glucosinolate biosynthesis
Genes.perturbation_type: CRISPR-Cas9 knockout of MYB34 and MYB51 in qs-2 background
Genes.quantitative_effect: qs-2myb3451 triple mutants lack indolic GSLs while retaining aliphatic GSLs (Fig. 2d); insect resistance retained—larvae grew small similar to qs-2 (Fig. 2e, f)
Genes.reaction_catalyzed: Transcription factors activating indolic GSL biosynthetic genes

Genes.biosynthesis_catabolism_transport_regula: Biosynthesis (ROS-generating enzyme; negative result—H2O2/O2⁻ do not contribute to JA-mediated GSL accumulation)
Genes.causal_chain_text: Mutation of RBOHF in qs-2 background (qs-2rbohF3) removed H2O2 and O2⁻ accumulation but did not affect JA biosynthesis or GSL hyperaccumulation. 1O2 remained elevated in qs-2rbohF3. This demonstrates that chloroplastic 1O2, not H2O2/O2⁻, is the ROS species responsible for activating JA biosynthesis and defense metabolism upon NAD+ deficiency.
Genes.directionality: RBOHF mutation in qs-2 → ↓H2O2 and O2⁻ but ↔JA, JA-Ile, 13-HPOT, 12-OPDA, GSLs remain at qs-2 levels; 1O2 still elevated
Genes.evidence_type: Genetic perturbation (loss-of-function) + targeted metabolite quantification + ROS detection (DAB/NBT staining, SOSG)
Genes.final_nutrient_products_affected: Glucosinolates (no effect on GSL levels when mutated in qs-2 background)
Genes.intermediate_metabolites_affected: H2O2, O2⁻ (removed in qs-2rbohF3); 1O2 still present; JA, JA-Ile, 13-HPOT, 12-OPDA unchanged vs qs-2
Genes.pathway: ROS (H2O2 and O2⁻) production at plasma membrane; NADPH oxidase
Genes.perturbation_type: Loss-of-function mutation rbohF3 in qs-2 background (qs-2rbohF3)
Genes.quantitative_effect: JA and JA biosynthetic precursors in qs-2rbohF3 remain at levels similar to qs-2 (Fig. 5a–e); GSLs unchanged (Fig. 5f); H2O2 and O2⁻ removed but 1O2 still enriched (Fig. 5h, i)
Genes.reaction_catalyzed: NADPH oxidase generating superoxide (O2⁻) and hydrogen peroxide (H2O2)

Title: NAD+ deficiency primes defense metabolism via 1O2-escalated jasmonate biosynthesis in plants
